text: Good afternoon. Thank you to SAGEs and the opportunity to present our case. We have no disclosures to make.

This is the case of a twenty-three-year-old woman who presented with a history of constipation for five years. Her symptoms were progressive and increasingly refractory to dietary changes, linacletide therapy, and laxative use. She reported at that time three bowel movements per week and substantial abdominal distension that limited her quality of life. Her past medical history was significant for hypothyroidism and vitiligo.

Physical exam and laboratory studies were unremarkable. Transenteric motility study demonstrated normal gastric and small bowel motility and moderately delayed colonic transit. Anal rectal manometry and MR defecography showed no pelvic floor dyssynergia, and colonoscopy was normal.

Given the chronicity of the patient's symptoms and their recalcitrance to medical therapy, She was scheduled for single incision laparoscopic subtotal colectomy. A mechanical bowel prep was given preoperatively.

The surgeon makes a three-centimeter transverse incision above the pubic symphysis, separating the rectus muscle at the midline on the linea alba. A wound protector and gel point access system are placed. The peritoneum over the iliocolic mesentery is scored, creating a window in the mesentery to isolate and divide the iliocolic pedicle using an energy device, ensuring the right ureter is identified. Medial to lateral mobilization of the ascending colon is performed, avoiding injury to the duodenum, followed by hepatic flexure mobilization using blunt dissection and energy devices. Lateral attachments are divided along the white line from the cecum to the hepatic flexure, continuing to the transverse colon.

The surgeon observes the medialized right colon adjacent to the pancreas and duodenum, proceeding to divide the greater omentum and transverse colon mesentery simultaneously. These structures are separated until reaching the splenic flexure, facilitated by the patient's thin body habitus and the port at the fan and steel incision.

The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon in a lateral to medial direction, following the line of Toldt to free the descending colon. The mesentery is divided using an energy device, starting proximally and moving distally to the point where the taenia coli converge on the sigmoid colon.

In the right lower quadrant, the surgeon visualizes the ureter and terminal ileum. The remaining attachments between the colon and abdominal wall are divided. The terminal ileum and cecum are delivered into the wound, and the terminal ileum is divided with a stay suture placed. The colon is exteriorized until reaching the mesocolon, with stay sutures placed through the tinea. ICG fluorescence is used to verify the blood supply to the remaining upper rectum.

The surgeon places a GIA stapler across the demarcation point and fires it. A hand-sewn side-to-end two-layered anastomosis is created between the terminal ileum and the upper rectum using Vicryl and silk sutures. The anastomosis is gently placed into the abdomen, followed by a final inspection to ensure hemostasis and to check for mesenteric twisting.

and her quality of life had greatly improved as she reported increased exercise tolerance and a noticeable improvement in the anxiety pertaining to her bowel habits. Final pathology returned as total abdominal colectomy with scattered T cells within the myenteric plexus consistent with myenteric lymphocytic plexitis. Colonic inertia is defined as the inability to modify stool to acceptable consistency and to move stool from cecum to recto sigmoid at least once every three days. The majority of patients with this condition are female and under forty years of age.

Surgery is indicated for patients with persistent symptoms despite management with medicines, biofeedback, and dietary modification. For patients with chronic constipation, delayed transit, and intact pelvic floor function, subtotal colectomy with iliorectal anastomosis is the operation that's associated with superior results. The majority of patients undergoing this procedure for this diagnosis have improved quality of life parameters that are sustained in the long term. And preservation of the rectum can help minimize the incidence of urgency and incontinence. And then bowel movement consistency gradually will improve with intestinal adaptation.

While single incision laparoscopic colectomy is typically performed through a midline approach, we've demonstrated its feasibility through a fan and steel incision. Such a technique requires the patient's body habit is to be of sufficient length to permit an ergonomic case, particularly for the mobilization of the flexures, though not excessively lengthy, which would render the fan and steel port too far from the operative field. Regular coordination of positioning with the anesthesiologist can assist with mobilization and dissection. Our case demonstrates that such an approach is safe, ergonomically feasible, and likely associated with a lower incidence of incisional hernia when compared to single incision laparoscopic collecting performed through the midline.

Finally, and perhaps most important to the patient, the resulting scar is more cosmetically sound than that which results from a midline approach. We have since duplicated the approach in another patient with the same underlying diagnosis, and their well-healed incisions are seen here side by side. Thank you.
